div.head: 240. Memorandum From the Department of State Executive Secretary (Brubeck) to the President’s Special Assistant for National Security Affairs (Bundy)Source: Department of State, Central Files, AID (US) UAR. Limited Official Use. Drafted by Barrow on May 2 and cleared by Grant, Dutton, Folger, Greenfield, Williams and Gaud (AID), and Strong. A May 12 note attached to the original of this memorandum reads: “General Clifton said the President has seen the attached.” (Kennedy Library, National Security Files, Countries Series, United Arab Republic, 4/63–5/63)

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item: Justification of US Aid to the UAR

div.p: Pursuant to a request from Mr. Komer,An unsigned May 1 memorandum, presumably by Komer, requested the Department of State to prepare by May 2 a memorandum that would deal with charges voiced in Congress and by Zionist groups that U.S. aid was helping the UAR in its conflict with Israel. The Department of State memorandum was to cite all arguments against this charge and provide statistics on both public and private military and economic assistance to the UAR. (Ibid., NEA/NE Files: Lot 66 D 5, White House Memoranda) we enclose a summary justification of United States aid to the UAR. Detailed statements of aid and contributions of all types and from all sources to Israel and the UAR are attached.No attachments are filed with the source text. They are appended to a copy of the memorandum ibid.
Classified materials, and arguments better not put in writing, for use in briefing Congressmen, are being prepared and will follow shortly.

signed.note: Read signed for Brubeck above Brubeck’s typed signature.

frus:attachment.head: EnclosureNo classification marking.
frus:attachment.p: JUSTIFICATION OF US AID TO THE UAR

p.hi: I. UAR Policy Only Understandable in Context US Near East Policy Principal Interests of US in Near East

list.label: A.
list.item: Limit Soviet influence and prevent Soviet domination.
list.label: B.
list.item: Maintain intercourse with area and use of communications routes across it, e.g. Suez.
list.label: C.
list.item: Assure Israel’s well being.
list.label: D.
list.item: Insure access to oil on reasonable terms.
list.label: E.
list.item: Adequate identification with modernizing forces; growth of free stable societies.

p.hi: II. Principal Threats to Attainment of US Interests

list.label: A.
list.item: Poverty and chaos of Near East which constitutes fertile feuding ground for indigenous Communists and anti-Western nationalists.
list.label: B.

item.p: Whenever these disputes reach such a boiling point that USG feels compelled to intervene forcefully and openly to one side, e.g. Israel or one particular Arab group as in 1958, Soviets and other anti-US forces have great opportunity to expand their influence in other NE states. USG must retain its present moderating capacity with all principal forces in NE; impossible if US-UAR openly hostile to each other.

list.label: C.
list.item: Understandable heritage of Arab distrust Western policies toward Arab Near East.

p.hi: III. Benefits to US from Aid to UAR

list.label: A.
list.item: Opportunity to discuss issues frankly and influence UAR policy (few non-negotiable issues).
list.label: B.
list.item: Limit Soviet influence.
list.label: C.
list.item: Minimum agitation and action against vital Western interests.
list.label: D.
list.item: Absence of incidents against US citizens and installations.
list.label: E.
list.item: Non-activation of Arab-Israel problem and cooperation with UN peace-keeping machinery.
list.label: F.
list.item: True neutrality by UAR on global and cold war issues.
list.label: G.
list.item: Installation of Western technology and cultural influence in UAR.
list.label: H.
list.item: Increased interrelationship of UAR with free world in manifold economic, cultural, scientific and other affairs.
list.label: I.
list.item: Modification of anti-US propaganda and favorable treatment of US aid in UAR news media, setting tone for rest of Arab world.
list.label: J.
list.item: Non-discrimination in issuance of visas.
list.label: K.
list.item: Continued UAR disposition to suppress local Communist parties in Arab world and Africa.
list.label: L.
list.item: Increased commercial opportunities for US throughout area.

p.hi: IV. Effects on US Interests if Aid Cut Off

list.label: A.
list.item: Would be considered hostile act and UAR would have to react strongly—another “Aswan Dam Incident”.
list.label: B.
list.item: Many years would be required to restore confidence and reinstitute dialogue.
list.label: C.
list.item: Soviets would be greatly strengthened in their efforts to drive Western influence and interests out of the Near East. Local Communist parties would grow.
list.label: D.
list.item: Would cause UAR to side with USSR on cold war issues.
list.label: E.
list.item: Western oil interests would be placed under greater pressure.
list.label: F.
list.item: Would greatly heighten Arab-Israel tensions.
list.label: G.
list.item: “Hate Americans” campaign would unleash fanatics throughout Arab area.
list.label: H.
list.item: Would not lessen UAR military capacity vis-á-vis Israel.

p.hi: V. Implications of Cairo Unity Proclamation (4/17)

list.label: A.
list.item: Proclamation of unity, which not legal document, contained reference in two of about 125 paragraphs to “liberation of Palestine” and opposition to “Zionism and imperialism”; proclamation itself clearly directed toward inter-Arab matters; aforementioned passages standard formulation of long-standing Arab position.
list.label: B.
list.item: Despite provision for effort establish unified command, proclamation envisages regional control of armies for indefinite future; armies geographically separated; physical integration not repeat not envisaged.
list.label: C.
list.item: Building of solid federation is long-term task. Cannot be done in context of war against Israel.
list.label: D.
list.item:
Establishment of Federation will increase pressure on Jordan but several countervailing factors against serious political change there. 1.
Courage and leadership of Husayn. 2.
Loyalty of Jordanian army. 3.
Economic and political liabilities which Jordan would entail for federation. 4.
Possible Israel reaction. 5
Publicly declared US interest in integrity of Jordan as well as other states of area.
list.label: E.
list.item: Iraq’s entry in the Federation increases pressure on Kuwait but latter protected by defense tie with UK and by general Arab desire that Iraq not obtain Kuwaiti revenues for exclusively Iraqi use.
list.label: F.
list.item: Success of disengagement efforts in Yemen, declared US interest in integrity of Saudi Arabia, Saudi Arabia’s special internal circumstances (diverse provincial structure and tribal society) makes situation manageable in Saudi Arabia.

p.hi: VI. Factors Bearing on Israel’s Security

list.label: A.
list.item: Israel militarily superior to combined Arab forces. Internal lines of communication and tight central control are assets Arab armies would not enjoy.
list.label: B.
list.item: Danger of surprise attack reduced by Hawk surface-to-air missiles.
list.label: C.
list.item: In event threat to peace in area, international factors loom larger than local forces as demonstrated Suez 1956 and Lebanon-Jordan in 1958.
list.label: D.
list.item: UAR has higher priority objectives than attacking Israel, e.g., strengthening position in Arab world and building economy for which Western aid required.
list.label: E.
list.item: UAR clearly believes that if it starts war with Israel it would be engaging itself in war of unknown proportions which it could not win and in which there would be external intervention.
list.label: F.
list.item: Doubtful Soviets wish UAR attack Israel since might force them to unwelcome confrontation with West.
list.label: G.
list.item: Activities of German and other Europeans in UAR aircraft industry adds no significant new military dimension; both parties can obtain all the jets they want; reduces UAR reliance on USSR in this field; manned aircraft declining in significance; effective defenses in being; West can control sources of materials and parts if need be.
list.label: H.
list.item: Production ground-to-ground rockets of more serious concern but do not pose imminent threat view absence guidance system, small number, 500 lb. conventional warheads. No prospect for other types of warheads. We actively seeking more information and examining possible courses of action. Frontal public challenge to UAR would not serve useful purpose since we would lose any ability to influence.
list.label: I.
list.item: No evidence Germans involved are ex-Nazis.

p.hi: VII. Effect of US Aid on UAR Arms Purchases and Programs

list.label: A.
list.item: Bulk of UAR armaments comes from Soviets repayable by cotton exports undisposable in Western markets at reasonable prices.
list.label: B.
list.item: UAR obtained quantities of arms prior to institution of sizable US aid program.
list.label: C.
list.item: The input of any significant amount of US aid resources for UAR economic development began in UAR FY 1959. The UAR’s development budget has since increased from $132 million in FY 1959 to $281 million in FY 1960, and to $756 million in FY 1962. The input of US resources for development purposes in FY 1962 equaled 24% of the UAR development budget.
list.label: D.
list.item: In the past three years, UAR development budgets have increased by 300%, defense budgets by about 40%. In the FY 1962 UAR budget, the development budget accounts for 42% of the total, the defense budget for 15%.
list.label: E.
list.item: UAR armed forces are not out of proportion for nation of 27,000,000 (about 100,000-120,000).
list.label: F.
list.item: US aid mostly in form of surplus foodstuffs—balance is to carefully selected development projects tied to US procurement.
list.label: G.
list.item: UAR’s Yemen campaign conducted mostly with Soviet supplies; financed largely with local currency; believe no significant drawdown of foreign exchange essential to development program.
list.label: H.
list.item: IMF stabilization loan, in which US participated, designed to correct effects of bad harvest which long preceded Yemen conflict. US aid not diverted to Yemen campaign.

list.label: A.
list.item: UAR’s activist policies have created and likely continue create problems in area.
list.label: B.
list.item: Some of these problems would occur anyway as forces of modernization take root and gain strength.
list.label: C.
list.item: US and West must learn to adjust to new situations if to stay ahead of Soviets in cold war game.
list.label: D.
list.item: Problems created by UAR thus far manageable, through combination urging restraint on Cairo and strengthening affected areas; would become far less manageable if US voluntarily destroys diplomatic leverage by adopting hostile policy toward UAR.
list.label: E.
list.item: Though by no means a cure-all, economic cooperation is backbone of long-term US policy which has gradually demonstrated significant accomplishments. US can continue to effect improvements through it provided we do not expect miracles overnight.
